heading: API Specializations
content: All QNN HTP backend specialization headers live under <QNN_SDK_ROOT>/include/QNN/HTP/. This section documents everything specific to the HTP backend (as opposed to generic QNN APIs).

heading: API Usage Guidelines
content: Minimum-expected and expanded HTP backend API callflows are documented on the separate 'HTP API Usage Guidelines' page (referenced but not inlined here).

heading: Usage Expectations
content: Two hard usage rules: (1) calls to QnnGraph_addNode() to build the QNN model must follow node dependency order; (2) QnnBackend_registerOpPackage() takes an optional 'target' parameter with values 'CPU' (both Linux x86 and ARM op packages), 'HTP' (op package loaded on the HTP), or nullptr (on ARM, loads the registered HTP op package when loading a context binary; on Linux x86, registers the x86 op package). Loading a context binary generated for a different HTP arch than the target device may give indeterminate results.

heading: QNN HTP Supported Operations
content: QNN HTP supports quantized 8-bit and quantized 16-bit networks on all Qualcomm SoCs (see the 'Backend Support HTP' column in Supported Operations). It supports float32-networks-run-as-float16-math on select SoCs (see 'Backend Support HTP FP16' column, if the SDK build supports it). It supports bfloat16 networks on Qualcomm Windows V81-and-newer platforms (see 'Backend Support HTP BF16' column, if the SDK build supports it).

heading: QNN HTP Supported Core Types
content: QNN HTP supports multiple core types on select SoCs: NSPSS and HPASS. Clients query platform capabilities via QnnDevice_getPlatformInfo(), which returns a QnnDevice_PlatformInfo_t containing per-core QnnHtpDevice_t core-type info. A device handle for a specific core type/ID is created by populating a customized QnnDevice_Config_t (option QNN_DEVICE_CONFIG_OPTION_PLATFORM_INFO) filtered to the desired core.coreType (e.g. QNN_HTP_CORE_TYPE_HPASS) and passing it to QnnDevice_create(). For offline HTP backend-extension config, core selection uses the devices[].core_type field (0 = NSP default, 1 = HPASS) plus core_id. HPASS cores have specific limitations vs NSPSS on HVX/HMX thread counts, VTCM sizes, and FP16 support availability.

heading: QNN HTP Variable Batch
content: QNN HTP supports variable batch dimension in a limited manner: the batch dimension at graph execute time can be any positive integer multiple of the batch dimension provided at graph prepare time, and all input/output tensors must share the same multiple. E.g. if prepare-time tensor dims are [b,h,w,d], execute can use [n*b,h,w,d] for any positive integer n.

heading: QNN HTP Backend API — Device and Context Custom Config Options
content: QnnHtpDevice_CustomConfig_t options (set via QNN_DEVICE_CONFIG_OPTION_CUSTOM): QNN_HTP_DEVICE_CONFIG_OPTION_SOC sets target SoC model (default QNN_SOC_MODEL_SM8350; Qnn_SocModel_t is deprecated in favor of the Supported Snapdragon Devices list); QNN_HTP_DEVICE_CONFIG_OPTION_ARCH sets HTP arch (default QNN_HTP_DEVICE_ARCH_NONE) — if both SOC and ARCH are set, the driver uses SOC and ignores ARCH (SOC is recommended); QNN_HTP_DEVICE_CONFIG_OPTION_SIGNEDPD enables signed process domain (default false/unsigned; requires pushing a signed DSP image to target). QnnHtpContext_CustomConfig_t options (set via QNN_CONTEXT_CONFIG_OPTION_CUSTOM, applied at QnnContext_createFromBinary/create): QNN_HTP_CONTEXT_CONFIG_OPTION_WEIGHT_SHARING_ENABLED (bool) — only supports offline prepare on x86_Linux, only Hexagon v73+, only within a single PD, max 64 shared graphs per context, old binaries must be regenerated to benefit; QNN_HTP_CONTEXT_CONFIG_OPTION_REGISTER_MULTI_CONTEXTS with QnnHtpContext_GroupRegistration_t{firstGroupHandle, maxSpillFillBuffer} lets multiple contexts share a max spill-fill buffer size (must be >0) — first context passes firstGroupHandle=0x0 (new group), subsequent contexts pass the first context's handle; QNN_HTP_CONTEXT_CONFIG_OPTION_REGISTER_CONCURRENT_RESOURCE_SHARING is the analogous option for concurrent (not just sequential) spill-fill and VTCM-backup buffer sharing — Android-only, V81 Hexagon architecture only, requires all contexts in a group to share the same priority (not modifiable on the fly via QnnContext_setConfig/QnnGraph_setConfig while sharing is enabled). Use qnn-context-binary-utility (search 'spillFillBufferSize' key) or parse QnnSystemContext.h/QnnHtpSystemContext.h structs at runtime to determine the max spill-fill buffer size needed across contexts before deserializing.

heading: QNN HTP Backend API — Additional Context Config Options
content: QNN_HTP_CONTEXT_CONFIG_OPTION_FILE_READ_MEMORY_BUDGET (fileReadMemoryBudgetInMb) loads the serialized binary in chunks of the given MB size (min(fileSize, budget) used if budget exceeds file size; 0 disables the feature). QNN_HTP_CONTEXT_CONFIG_OPTION_IO_MEM_ESTIMATION (ioMemoryEstimation bool) enables I/O memory estimation. QNN_HTP_CONTEXT_CONFIG_OPTION_SHARE_RESOURCES (shareResources bool) plus QNN_HTP_CONTEXT_CONFIG_OPTION_SHARE_RESOURCES_OPTIMIZATION_TYPE (shareResOptType, only applied when share_resources is true) select among three group-configuration optimization types: SEQUENTIAL_WITH_VA_OPTIMIZATION (graphs must execute sequentially; optimizes both HTP virtual-address space and runtime memory; ideal for large multi-split generative-AI workloads; VA optimization only on specific SoCs, else returns QNN_CONTEXT_ERROR_UNSUPPORTED_FEATURE), SEQUENTIAL_WITHOUT_VA_OPTIMIZATION (sequential execution required, optimizes runtime memory only, suited to smaller/traditional models), and CONCURRENT_OPTIMIZATION (concurrent execution with shared spill-fill/VTCM-backup buffers across same-priority contexts; Android + V81 Hexagon only). QNN_HTP_CONTEXT_CONFIG_OPTION_INIT_ACCELERATION (initAcceleration bool) speeds up context init. QNN_HTP_CONTEXT_CONFIG_OPTION_CONCURRENT_DESERIALIZATION_PATCH (concurrentDeserializationPatchFd) supplies a read-write patch file FD used for concurrent deserialization. QNN_HTP_CONTEXT_CONFIG_OPTION_SKIP_VALIDATION_ON_BINARY_SECTION (bool) skips binary-section validation. QNN_HTP_CONTEXT_CONFIG_OPTION_LORA_WEIGHT_SHARING_ENABLED (loraWeightSharingEnabled bool) — offline prepare on x86_Linux only; old binaries need regeneration to benefit. QNN_HTP_CONTEXT_CONFIG_OPTION_REUSED_IO_LIMIT (reusedIoLimitMb) sets reused I/O buffer size in MB. QNN_HTP_CONTEXT_CONFIG_OPTION_GRAPH_SPLITTING_ENABLED (graphSplittingEnabled bool, set via QnnContext_create not createFromBinary) — offline prepare on x86_Linux only; models must be re-prepared to benefit, old binaries unaffected.

heading: QNN HTP Backend API — Graph Config Options and Device Info Extensions
content: QnnHtpGraph_CustomConfig_t options (QnnHtpGraph.h, paired with file_include_QNN_QnnGraph.h): QNN_HTP_GRAPH_CONFIG_OPTION_OPTIMIZATION selects HTP graph optimization type (default QNN_HTP_GRAPH_OPTIMIZATION_TYPE_UNKNOWN). QNN_HTP_GRAPH_CONFIG_OPTION_PRECISION selects math precision (default QNN_PRECISION_FLOAT32; deprecated starting SDK 2.35 — no longer needs to be set). QNN_HTP_GRAPH_CONFIG_OPTION_VTCM_SIZE_IN_MB / _VTCM_SIZE reserves VTCM (default 4 MB; set to QNN_HTP_GRAPH_CONFIG_OPTION_MAX plus a target SoC via QNN_HTP_DEVICE_CONFIG_OPTION_SOC to use the device max; STRICT binary-compatibility mode performs an optimality check on VTCM=MAX binaries). QNN_HTP_GRAPH_CONFIG_OPTION_FOLD_RELU_ACTIVATION_INTO_CONV_OFF (default: always fold) disables folding Relu/ReluMinMax into a preceding Conv, at a performance cost, when clients can't guarantee the Relu's quantization range exactly matches post-Relu data. QNN_HTP_GRAPH_CONFIG_OPTION_SHORT_DEPTH_CONV_ON_HMX_OFF (default: always use HMX) should be set when weights are asymmetric with short-depth convolutions, to guarantee accuracy. QNN_HTP_GRAPH_CONFIG_OPTION_HIGH_PRECISION_SIGMOID (default false) trades performance for accuracy using an FP16 high-precision HVX Sigmoid kernel. QNN_HTP_GRAPH_CONFIG_OPTION_NUM_HVX_THREADS (default 4) reserves HVX threads for a graph. QNN_HTP_GRAPH_CONFIG_OPTION_WEIGHTS_PACKING (default false, experimental beta) packs 8-bit weights that fall in the 4-bit range as packed 4-bit in the context binary, potentially shrinking binary size (no guaranteed perf gain). QNN_HTP_GRAPH_CONFIG_OPTION_FINALIZE_CONFIG sets graph finalize-level settings (used for P-points, see below). QNN_HTP_GRAPH_CONFIG_OPTION_MONOLITHIC_LSTM (default false) runs LSTM as one monolithic unit instead of expanding into sub-ops. QNN_HTP_GRAPH_CONFIG_OPTION_PRECISION_COMPENSATION (default false, requires min_arch>=81) improves numerical precision for 16-bit-activation models on Hexagon v81+. Optimization level O=3 additionally factors in QNN_HTP_DEVICE_CONFIG_OPTION_SOC when available, generally yielding a more optimal (but possibly larger and slower-loading) graph. If both SOC and ARCH device config are set, SOC wins. QNN HTP Device Info Extension Options (QnnHtpDevice_DeviceInfoExtension_t, populated by the client for offline operation or by QnnDevice_getPlatformInfo() for online): QNN_HTP_DEVICE_CONFIG_OPTION_PCIE_DEVICE_INFO_EXTENSION and QNN_HTP_DEVICE_CONFIG_OPTION_ON_CHIP_DEVICE_INFO_EXTENSION both report VTCM size (MB), socModel, signed-PD support, DLBC support, and device architecture (PCIe variant additionally reports NSP count on the PCIe device).

heading: QNN HTP Performance Infrastructure API — DCVS_V3 Power Configuration
content: After loading the HTP library, clients call QnnDevice_getInfrastructure() then use QnnHtpPerfInfrastructure.h methods to control CPU/HTP system performance settings: voting policy (core clocks/voltage corners), DCVS modes, and RPC Control Latency (latency-critical apps should vote >0 and <200us; moderate apps can use >200us or the 0us default). QNN Perf Infrastructure maps to FastRPC on the CPU side and HAP_Power DCVS v3 on the HTP side; thread-count configuration for the accelerator is not supported here (use Hexagon SDK HAP_power_set API docs for voltage-corner/DCVS-mode details). A 'DCVS_V3 vote' (this doc's term, also called 'core vote'/'HVX vote' in older material) programs QDSP6 core voltage corner and HVX clock corner together via one struct — the two cannot be voted independently. QnnHtpPerfInfrastructure_DcvsV3_t fields: setBusParams/busVoltageCornerMin/Target/Max, setCoreParams/coreVoltageCornerMin/Target/Max, setDcvsEnable/dcvsEnable, setSleepLatency/sleepLatency (10-65535us), setSleepDisable/sleepDisable, powerMode (e.g. QNN_HTP_PERF_INFRASTRUCTURE_POWERMODE_PERFORMANCE_MODE). API workflow: createPowerConfigId(deviceId, coreId, &powerConfigId) associates a client context; setPowerConfig(powerConfigId, configs[]) applies one or more QnnHtpPerfInfrastructure_PowerConfig_t entries (option=QNN_HTP_PERF_INFRASTRUCTURE_POWER_CONFIGOPTION_DCVS_V3 plus dcvsV3Config); destroyPowerConfigId(powerConfigId) removes all associated votes.

heading: QNN HTP Performance Infrastructure API — RPC Polling, HMX and CENG Votes
content: RPC Polling/Latency (not supported on QNX): QNN_HTP_PERF_INFRASTRUCTURE_POWER_CONFIGOPTION_RPC_CONTROL_LATENCY (rpcControlLatencyConfig, recommended 100us) and _RPC_POLLING_TIME (rpcPollingTimeConfig, 0-10000us recommended, e.g. 9999) are set via the same setPowerConfig call pattern. Adaptive Polling (QNN_HTP_PERF_INFRASTRUCTURE_POWER_CONFIGOPTION_ADAPTIVE_POLLING_TIME, adaptivePollingTimeConfig) only activates if RPC polling is already enabled, and is not recommended for models with <1ms inference time — it sets a minimum inference-time threshold before polling activates, saving CPU power by skipping unnecessary polling. HMX Power Settings (QNN_HTP_PERF_INFRASTRUCTURE_POWER_CONFIGOPTION_HMX_V2, hmxV2Config: hmxPickDefault, hmxPerfMode, hmxVoltageCornerMin/Target/Max) — Hexagon v75+ only; requires an active DCVS_V3 (or DCVS_V3_EXP) vote on the same powerConfigId or the HMX vote is denied with INVALID_INPUT; if no HMX vote is given, a default is applied; once an explicit HMX vote is placed, the client must set hmxPickDefault and call setPowerConfig again to revert to default; destroyPowerConfigId removes all associated votes. CENG (Compression Engine, handles weight/activation compression) Power Settings (QNN_HTP_PERF_INFRASTRUCTURE_POWER_CONFIGOPTION_CENG, cengConfig) have identical prerequisite/default rules to HMX — Hexagon v75+ only, requires an active DCVS_V3 vote, default is QNN_HTP_PERF_INFRASTRUCTURE_CLK_PERF_HIGH with DCVS_VOLTAGE_VCORNER_MIN_VOLTAGE_CORNER. One setPowerConfig call can bundle DCVS_V3 + HMX_V2 + CENG configs together via a single powerConfigs[] array.

heading: QNN HTP Performance Infrastructure API — Expanded Voltage Corners (V85+)
content: Hexagon v85 introduces finer-grained voltage/perf control via two new setPowerConfig options: QNN_HTP_PERF_INFRASTRUCTURE_POWER_CONFIGOPTION_DCVS_V3_EXP (expanded core/bus corners + perf mode) and _CENG_EXP (expanded CENG corners + perf mode). Use expanded corners for sustained workloads needing a throughput/thermal sweet spot finer than legacy coarse corners (SVS/NOM/TURBO), for battery-sensitive apps wanting the lowest corner meeting a latency target, or when a SKU maps one voltage corner to multiple physical frequencies. Two axes: ExpVoltageCorner_t values (DCVS_EXP_VCORNER_*, all >=0x100, e.g. NOM, NOM_L0, TUR_L3) select the voltage rail; ClkPerfMode_t values (HIGH, HIGH_D1..HIGH_D15, LOW) select among frequencies mapped to that corner (relative selector, not an absolute-frequency guarantee — hardware clamps to nearest supported). No dedicated capability-query API exists; clients discover support by sending DCVS_V3_EXP/CENG_EXP and checking for QNN_HTP_PERF_INFRASTRUCTURE_ERROR_UNSUPPORTED (returned instantly pre-v85 via host-side arch gate with no FastRPC traffic; returned once-per-PD and cached thereafter on v85+ with an older CDSP image) — the recommended pattern is try-expanded-then-fall-back-to-legacy on that error. DcvsV3Exp_t mirrors DcvsV3_t but has no setDcvsEnable/dcvsEnable (DCVS is implicit when EXP is voted) and adds corePerfMode/busPerfMode fields; all six corner fields must be 0 (DCVS_EXP_VCORNER_DISABLE) or an ExpVoltageCorner_t (>=0x100) — legacy values return INVALID_INPUT. DCVS_V3_EXP cannot combine with legacy DCVS_V3 in the same call (INVALID_INPUT); across separate calls the most recent variant supersedes the other. DCVS_V3_EXP may freely combine with HMX_V2, DDR_PERF_MODE, legacy CENG, and CENG_EXP. QNN automatically replays the most recent DCVS_V3_EXP vote after a CDSP SubSystem Restart if the recovered architecture still supports it; otherwise the client gets ERROR_UNSUPPORTED on the next call and must re-issue with legacy corners. CENG_EXP requires an active DCVS_V3 or DCVS_V3_EXP vote first; cannot combine with legacy CENG in one call. DDR_PERF_MODE requires an active DCVS_V3/DCVS_V3_EXP vote with all three bus-corner fields at the applicable MAX value (DCVS_VOLTAGE_VCORNER_MAX_VOLTAGE_CORNER for legacy, DCVS_EXP_VCORNER_MAX for expanded).

heading: QNN HTP Precision
content: QNN HTP supports graphs mixing floating-point and fixed-point data types. On select SoCs, float32 graphs run using float16 math: the client sets up float32 tensors and the HTP accelerator finalizes/executes with float16 math, converting user float32 inputs to float16 internally and returning float32 outputs — QNN_HTP_GRAPH_CONFIG_OPTION_PRECISION is deprecated from release 2.35 onward (no longer needs setting). QNN HTP does not support true float32 math.

heading: QNN HTP FP16 output difference between SM8550 and SM8650
content: Floating-point model outputs on the HTP backend differ slightly between SM8550 and SM8650, due to hardware changes affecting the associativity of some computations for higher efficiency — neither device is more accurate than the other, but exact bit-for-bit reproducibility across the two SoCs should not be assumed. Also noted in the 2.9.1 release notes 'HTP Float16' slide.

heading: QNN HTP Deep Learning Bandwidth Compression (DLBC)
content: DLBC compresses inputs (and/or weights, independently configurable) to lower processing bandwidth. Runtime config: QnnHtpGraph_CustomConfig_t option QNN_HTP_GRAPH_CONFIG_OPTION_OPTIMIZATION with optimizationOption.type = QNN_HTP_GRAPH_OPTIMIZATION_TYPE_ENABLE_DLBC (inputs) or _ENABLE_DLBC_WEIGHTS (weights), floatValue 1.0 to enable / 0 to disable. Offline backend-extension config: 'dlbc' and 'dlbc_weights' keys in the graphs[] object (0 disables/default, >=1 enables). Limitations: offline preparation only; not supported with weight sharing or spill-fill buffer sharing; graph count cap depends on which compression is enabled — 32 graphs for input-OR-weight compression, 16 for input-AND-weight compression. DLBC Weights + Weight Sharing together is unsupported as of release 2.36 (binaries prepared before 2.36 with both enabled get DLBC Weights silently turned off; must re-prepare without Weight Sharing to use DLBC WTS) — except on select targets with hardware capability where both may be enabled together, and enabling DLBC weights on any graph in a weight-shared group enables it across all graphs sharing those weights.

heading: QNN HTP Sparse Weights Compression
content: Compresses sparse weight data to reduce DDR and VTCM memory footprint. Config: QnnHtpGraph_CustomConfig_t optimizationOption.type = QNN_HTP_GRAPH_OPTIMIZATION_TYPE_ENABLE_SPARSE_WEIGHTS_COMPRESSION (floatValue 1.0/0). Offline backend-extension config key 'sparse_weights_compression' (0 default/disabled, >=1 enabled); the pre-2.20 config nested it under a single 'graphs' object, replaced from SDK 2.20 onward by a 'graphs' array. Compression savings are reported in qnn-context-binary-generator output as 'spare weights compression bytes saved: ...'. Limitations: only supported on automotive Snapdragon devices SA8255 and SA8620P; offline prepare only.

heading: QNN UBWC (Universal Bandwidth Compression)
content: UBWC compresses image-format input/output tensors to improve effective system-memory throughput; no model-conversion changes are needed (graph is converted/quantized with uncompressed data). Enabled per-tensor at context-binary-generation time via the data-format config's 'dataFormat' field (e.g. QNN_TENSOR_DATA_FORMAT_UBWC_NV12_Y / _UV for split-plane NV12, or RGBA8888 variants), independently for input and output tensors. Limitations: only supported on select automotive SoCs; offline prepare only; only supported with shared buffers.

heading: QNN HTP - Setting Number of HVX Threads
content: More HVX threads generally lowers graph execution time. Configurable for both online and offline prepare via QnnHtpGraph_CustomConfig_t option QNN_HTP_GRAPH_CONFIG_OPTION_NUM_HVX_THREADS (numHvxThreads, max = number of HVX HW blocks on the SoC), or the offline backend-extension config key 'hvx_threads' in graphs[]. Thread count cannot be (re)configured after the graph's first execution — must be set beforehand. Offline: if 'hvx_threads' is omitted, a default of 4 is written into the binary blob. Online: if no thread count is configured, the max supported value for the SoC is used at inference time. QnnGraph_setConfig can re-configure the value prior to first execution.

heading: QNN HTP - Enabling the system level cache allocator
content: Enables the System Level Cache Allocator for a graph to save overall bandwidth. Config: QnnHtpGraph_CustomConfig_t optimizationOption.type = QNN_HTP_GRAPH_OPTIMIZATION_TYPE_ENABLE_SLC_ALLOCATOR (floatValue 1 to enable). Offline backend-extension config key 'slc_alloc_enable' (per-graph, plus device 'soc_id'). Only supported on specific SoCs; off by default; configurable for offline prepare only and cannot be modified mid-inference — though the feature can be force-disabled during execution by re-issuing the same custom config with floatValue 0, and restored by re-issuing with value 1.

heading: QNN HTP Backend Extensions — Config Schema
content: qnn-net-run is backend-agnostic; HTP-specific custom configs and performance modes are exposed via an extension shared library (libQnnHtpNetRunExtensions.so) plus a JSON config file, passed to qnn-net-run/qnn-context-binary-generator via --config_file. Minimal wrapper: {"backend_extensions": {"shared_library_path": ..., "config_file_path": ...}}. The backend config schema's 'graphs[]' object supports: graph_names (array, ties to QnnGraph_create name), vtcm_mb (integer; 0 = QNN_HTP_GRAPH_CONFIG_OPTION_MAX, device max — pair with a device soc_id), hvx_threads (integer), O (optimization level, valid values 2 or 3 — level 1 is internal-only; higher O = longer prepare time, more optimal graph for most graphs), dlbc (0/1), weights_packing (bool, default false), num_cores (integer, default 1, offline-prepare only), short_depth_conv_on_hmx_off (bool, default false), fold_relu_activation_into_conv_off (bool, default false), advanced_activation_fusion (bool, default true — fuses Conv with sigmoid/tanh/gelu/swish for perf in float models at accuracy cost when disabled; no effect on quantized graphs), use_high_precision_fp16_sigmoid (bool, default false), monolithic_lstm (bool, default false). The 'devices[]' object supports device_id, core_id (array), core_type (0=NSP default, 1=HPASS), soc_id (deprecated, use soc_model), soc_model, dsp_arch (string), pd_session (default 'unsigned'), profiling_level, use_client_context (bool, unsupported v68+), and nested 'cores[]' with perf_profile (string, default 'high_performance'; overridden by qnn-net-run's --perf-profile CLI flag if given), rpc_control_latency (int, default 100us), rpc_polling_time (int, default 9999us for burst/high_performance/sustained_high_performance, else 0), hmx_timeout_us (int, default 300000us), adaptive_polling_time (int, default 0us). A top-level 'context' object supports weight_sharing_enabled, max_spill_fill_buffer_for_group + group_id (group_id must be 0 for max_spill_fill_buffer_for_group to apply), file_read_memory_budget_in_mb, io_memory_estimation, init_acceleration, concurrent_deserialization_patch (string path), lora_weight_sharing, lora_weight_sharing_ram_preload, reused_io_limit_mb. A 'groupContext' object supports share_resources (bool) and reused_io_limit_mb for multi-context binary lists. A 'memory' object supports mem_type: 'shared_buffer' for multi-tensor shared I/O buffers. Note: fp16_relaxed_precision is deprecated from 2.35.0 (determined automatically per SoC support).

heading: QNN HTP Backend Extensions — Traditional Performance Profiles
content: Backend-extension 'perf_profile' values (highest to lowest performance): burst, sustained_high_performance, high_performance, balanced, low_balanced, high_power_saver, power_saver, low_power_saver, extreme_power_saver, system_settings, plus the llm_decode_* family (see LLM Decode Modes). Each profile is a fixed combination of sleepLatency, dcvsEnable, RPC-polling on/off, and bus/core voltage-corner min/target/max. burst and sustained_high_performance use a timer during execution to hold votes high across inferences (avoiding repeated up/down voting until timeout), have low sleep latency (40us/100us), RPC polling on, DCVS off, and the MAX/TURBO voltage corners — burst is the single highest-performance setting. high_performance does not sustain votes between inferences (drops to idle RELAXED_POWER_STATE between calls, reducing CPU power) though it still uses TURBO corners and 100us sleep latency during execution. power_saver/low_power_saver/high_power_saver use low frequencies, high sleep latencies (1000us), and drop to idle RELEASED_POWER_STATE between inferences. extreme_power_saver is the lowest-performing/most power-saving mode (DISABLE corners, 2000us sleep latency). RELAXED_POWER_STATE/RELEASED_POWER_STATE are applied automatically per-profile between inferences and are not directly configurable. Graph execution has three stages (INIT, INFERENCE, DE-INIT); the selected performance mode's votes apply to each stage, then a lower idle vote applies after each stage per the selected mode.

heading: LLM Decode Performance Modes (llm_decode_*)
content: New in SDK 2.48; supported only on Hexagon V79+ (V79, V81, V85, V89) — selecting an llm_decode_* mode on older architectures is rejected at runtime. Rationale: LLM prefill is compute-bound (heavy HMX/HVX use, well-served by traditional modes that scale all clocks together), while LLM decode (autoregressive, one token at a time against the full KV-cache, batch=1 narrow matmuls) is memory-bandwidth-bound — HMX is underutilized. llm_decode_* modes decouple HMX frequency from DDR bandwidth: HMX is reduced aggressively across all tiers, DDR (bus) and HVX (core) are scaled per tier, CENG is tuned per tier, and 'DDR Perf Mode' (extra DDR cycles) is enabled only for llm_decode_burst — improving tokens-per-watt vs. traditional modes. Not recommended for prefill/encode, Large Vision Models, or other non-LLM-decode workloads — use traditional modes for those until dedicated profiles exist. Modes highest-to-lowest: llm_decode_burst, llm_decode_sustained_high_performance, llm_decode_high_performance, llm_decode_balanced, llm_decode_low_balanced, llm_decode_high_power_saver, llm_decode_power_saver, llm_decode_low_power_saver, llm_decode_extreme_power_saver, llm_decode_default (alias for llm_decode_balanced). Three tiers: high-performance (burst/sustained/high_performance — burst votes highest corners plus DDR Perf Mode ON, others hold elevated votes without the extra DDR boost); balanced (balanced/low_balanced/default — disables HMX, drops bus corner to NOM, trades throughput for tokens-per-watt); power-saver (high/low/extreme_power_saver — progressively drops HMX, HVX, and DDR for battery-bound/background inference). Set via backend-extension config 'perf_profile': 'llm_decode_burst' (etc.) exactly like traditional profiles.

heading: QNN HTP Profiling — Basic Profiling and DSP Heap Profiling
content: Basic profiling gives a host+accelerator graph-inference summary. DSP heap profiling (for QnnContext_createFromBinary use-cases) reports total DSP memory use at two points: before any context is created, and after all contexts are freed. Enable via QnnHtpContext_CustomConfig_t option QNN_HTP_CONTEXT_CONFIG_OPTION_DSP_MEMORY_PROFILING_ENABLED (dspMemoryProfilingEnabled bool) passed to the relevant QnnContext_createFromBinary call — must be enabled for both the first context created and the last context freed to get both readings. Values are retrieved from a QnnProfile_EventId_t/QnnProfile_EventData_t with identifier 'DSP:before_context_created' (via the profileHandle passed to the first createFromBinary) or 'DSP:after_context_freed' (via the profileHandle passed to the last QnnContext_free). If the config wasn't enabled for a given context, QnnContext_free won't output the metric. Limitations: Android and QNX only; enabling this may impact init/cleanup time.

heading: QNN HTP Profiling — Detailed and Linting Profiling
content: Detailed profiling reports per-op cost in cycle counts (not microseconds — no direct cycle-to-time conversion exists because of parallelized HTP execution); use per-layer cycle counts as a relative comparison metric only. HTP-specific 'linting' profiling additionally reports, per op: Wait (cycles waiting since the previous op finished, e.g. scheduling or background HVX/DMA), Overlap (cycles where at least one background op ran concurrently with this op on the main thread), Overlap (wait) (same but during the Wait period), and Resources (which of HVX/HMX/DMA the op used). Enable via qnn-net-run --profiling_level=backend plus a backend-specific config with devices[].profiling_level = 'linting'. View output with qnn-profile-viewer's libQnnHtpProfilingReader.so (raw per-run output) or libQnnChrometraceProfilingReader.so (averaged output, can also emit a chrometrace JSON via --output). Optimization workflow demonstrated on showcase models: an op with high total cycles and low Overlap fraction (little parallel background work) is a strong optimization target — replacing a costly Sub op with an equivalent Conv (manually designed weights) cut one showcase model's total NetRun time roughly in half and improved parallelism across all remaining ops; similarly replacing a Div op with an equivalent Mul op, and replacing PReLU ops with ReLU ops, are both called out as standard best-practice optimizations that reduce or eliminate a bottleneck op with poor background overlap.

heading: QNN HTP Optrace Profiling
content: Optrace produces a timeline chrometrace of op-level execution. Workflow: (1) Converter — optionally pass --export_format dlc and --enable_framework_trace so the chrometrace can show framework/QNN op-type names. (2) qnn-context-binary-generator — pass --profiling_level detailed --profiling_option optrace to emit a [MODEL]_schematic.bin alongside the context binary. (3) qnn-net-run — pass the same two flags to emit qnn-profiling-data.log during execution. (4) Post-process — run qnn-profile-viewer with --reader libQnnHtpOptraceProfilingReader.so, --input_log qnn-profiling-data.log, --schematic [MODEL]_schematic.bin, --output chrometrace.json; an optional --config config.json enables features: enable_input_output_flow_events, enable_sequencer_flow_events (both require the legacy chrometrace UI), htp_json (dumps [NAME]_htp.json — viewable directly in Netron, showing per-op HTP properties; default on), runtrace (execution/preemption events per core; default on), memory_info (VTCM/DRAM bandwidth + allocation graphs per core; default on), traceback (source-framework trace-back; default on), qhas_schema/qhas_json (dumps QHAS JSON, default off). Output chrometrace.json opens in Perfetto or chrome://tracing; a companion chrometrace_qnn_htp_analysis_summary.html QHAS report is also produced. --dlc [MODEL].dlc adds framework/QNN-op-type context; --zoom_start/--zoom_end restrict the trace to a named QNN-or-framework-node subnetwork. HTP ops classified as 'System Service' (not tied to a specific network op) include DramToTcm, TcmToDram, Sync Op, DmaCheckpointSet/Wait, BlockZapOp (zero-pads a tensor to block-size), and SystemService (prefetches into L2). Gzip compression (ratio observed ~0.05, e.g. 1300KB→60KB) is enabled via a .gz output filename, the --gzip flag, or the config.json 'gzip' key.

heading: QNN HTP Hextimate Profiling
content: Hextimate is an alternative to Optrace that estimates op performance rather than requiring a runtime trace — no qnn-net-run step is needed. Requires the same two context-binary-generator flags (--profiling_level detailed --profiling_option optrace) plus a config.json with a 'devices' entry specifying soc_model: 52 (required). Produces qnn-profiling-data.log and [MODEL]_schematic.bin directly from context-binary generation; the chrometrace is generated the same way as regular Optrace (qnn-profile-viewer + libQnnHtpOptraceProfilingReader.so). Hextimate profiling is only available on Auto SDK builds.

heading: QNN HTP Analysis Summary (QHAS)
content: QHAS is an HTML report auto-generated by qnn-profile-viewer as part of the normal Optrace flow (no extra parameters needed) that summarizes op-level data with analysis, as opposed to the chrometrace's visual timeline. Columns are sortable and can generate per-column pie charts; column values can be filtered by keyword (e.g. filtering 'HTP Op' by 'conv' and 'QNN Op' by 'batch' simultaneously). The 'Dominant Path' section shows the highest-priority HTP op over time, prioritized: HMX Op > HVX Op > ops performing DMA Reads > ops performing DMA Writes > DmaCheckpointSet/Wait > SyncOp. If the zoom_start/zoom_end submodule feature is used, QHAS analysis is restricted to that subnetwork. QHAS is Beta and subject to change.

heading: QNN Context Binary size
content: The QNN Context Binary (post-graph-preparation) is larger than the source QNN model for three reasons: (1) Number of Operations — HTP splits heavy ops into smaller pieces to fit VTCM and maximize parallelism, e.g. going from 30 to 300,000 ops at ~40 bytes/op grows the binary from 1.2KB to 12MB; (2) Sequencing and Data Paging — more ops means more stored metadata about execution order and which data must page between DDR and VTCM; (3) Constant Data — all graph constants (e.g. convolution filters) are embedded, sometimes padded to match HTP's internal tile format for performance, adding further size.

heading: Op Writing Guidelines — QHPI vs Legacy HTP Operator APIs
content: Two interfaces exist for custom HTP operators. General guidance: use QHPI whenever possible; QHPI and legacy op packages can coexist in one graph (incremental migration), cross-referencing each other via PackageName::OpName. Use QHPI when: forward compatibility matters (QHPI packages built on an older SDK keep working on newer SDKs without recompiling, via versioned APIs/structs — legacy packages must be recompiled every SDK release); writing a new op from scratch (QHPI's explicit C structs — QHPI_Kernel_vxxx, QHPI_Tensor_Signature_vxxx, QHPI_OpInfo_vxxx — reduce error surface vs. legacy's C++ macros); multi-threading is needed (QHPI has a first-class 'multithreaded' flag and slice APIs qhpi_num_slices/qhpi_slice_number); tiling needs are standard (QHPI's shape_required/shape_legalized/build_tile callbacks hook into the HTP central tiler, auto-balancing parallelism/TCM/inter-op cost); graph rewrites are straightforward (QHPI's early_rewrite/late_rewrite C callbacks with qhpi_op_create/qhpi_op_slice replace the DEF_PACKAGE_OPTIMIZATION DSL, covering the same pre/post-tiling phases more simply). Use legacy APIs when: a large existing legacy package makes migration not worth the cost; fine-grained optimization-pass-ordering control is needed (legacy DEF_PACKAGE_OPTIMIZATION accepts arbitrary numeric priorities like EARLY+1/MIDDLE/LATE+900, vs QHPI's two fixed phases); cost-based kernel selection is needed (legacy DEF_PACKAGE_OP_AND_COST_[F_]AND_FLAGS costs influence kernel selection at prepare time; QHPI costs only affect execution-time prediction, with kernel selection driven purely by tensor-signature matching order and predicates); complex multi-op pattern matching is needed (legacy DSL supports Op/OpVarIn/LET, RANK_OF/DIM_OF/CONSTVAL_INT/SAME_ENCODING/EXTERNAL_CONSTRAINT constraints, and AUTOSPLIT/TYPICAL_SLICE/CHANGEDIM_SLICE/OP_ITER/SHAPEFN_APPLY/EXTERNAL_REPLACE replacement patterns — more expressive than QHPI's callbacks for this case). QHPI is Beta and subject to change.

heading: Recommendations for Network Design
content: HTP supports A8, A16, and FP16 activations, with accuracy and power/energy cost ordered A8 < A16 < FP16 — to minimize power, try A8 first, escalate to A16 then FP16 only if accuracy requires it. Always use symmetric weight quantization on HTP for best accuracy; activation data should be asymmetric. Quantization-aware training (QAT) is recommended, especially for high-resolution image-transform models, since it can remove the need for 16-bit activations (letting 8-bit activations hit acceptable accuracy, improving both performance and power) — compare fakequant-model vs. original-float-model outputs in the original framework, and ensure fakequant nodes exist for all layers/kernels. A16W16 (int16 weights + uint16 activations) is supported for several Convolution-type ops on select SoCs (Conv2d, DepthConv2d, TransposeConv2D, FullyConnected, Matmul, Batchnorm, LayerNorm) — expected to beat A16W8 post-training-quantization accuracy and beat FP16 power efficiency at similar accuracy; all weights/filters must be symmetrically quantized (for Matmul specifically, Input A must be asymmetric and Input B symmetric). Hexagon hardware limits INT16 weights to the range 0x8000-0x7F7F (not the full 0x8000-0x7FFF) — pass --restrict_quantization_steps "-0x8000 0x7F7F" to the quantizer when using A16W16.

heading: Yielding, Parallel Graph Execution, VTCM Sharing, SSR (Pointers)
content: Four HTP concurrency/resilience features are documented on separate linked pages, not inlined in this manual section: Yielding and Pre-Emption (cooperative, user-based context switching — see 'HTP Yielding'); Parallel Graph Execution (available from v81 SoCs, lets more than one graph execute simultaneously — see 'HTP Parallel Graph Execution'); VTCM Sharing (from Hexagon v73, lets other threads in the same process share VTCM with QNN HTP — see 'HTP VTCM Sharing' and 'VTCM Windowing'); SubSystem Restart / SSR (an HTP-backend-specific feature letting the CDSP subsystem auto-restart an invalidated connection after a crash — see 'QNN HTP SSR').

heading: Running Model on Different HTP Devices (Auto)
content: qnn-net-run can target a specific HTP device (CDSP/NSP ID) via a --config_file JSON referencing the HTP net-extension library and a device_id (default 0 / CDSP0-NSP0 if no config given). Example config keys: backend_extensions.shared_library_path/config_file_path, plus a devices[] entry with dsp_arch and device_id (0 or 1 to select CDSP0/CDSP1). QNN HTP TARGET CONFIG TABLE maps automotive targets to dsp_arch/soc_id: SA8295→v68/39, SA8540→v68/62, SA8650→v73/52, SA8775→v73/52, SA8255→v73/52, SA8620→v75/67, SA7255→v75/67, SA8797→v81/72. Via the QNN API directly: call qnnInterface.deviceGetPlatformInfo() to retrieve QnnDevice_PlatformInfo_t (must be freed via deviceFreePlatformInfo(); unsupported on x86 Linux), search v1.hwDevices for the matching deviceId, populate a QnnDevice_Config_t (option QNN_DEVICE_CONFIG_OPTION_PLATFORM_INFO, hardwareInfo = the matched device struct with numHwDevices=1), and pass it to deviceCreate(). If no devConfig is given, device_id=0 (CDSP0/NSP0) is used by default here too.

heading: HTP Optimization (Auto) — Optimization Levels and P Points
content: Optimization level O=3 (set via backend-extension config 'O' key in graphs[], or QNN_HTP_GRAPH_OPTIMIZATION_TYPE_FINALIZE_OPTIMIZATION_FLAG=3 via the C API) may yield the most optimal graph but is not guaranteed best for every model — experimentation is required; specifying the correct target 'soc_id'/'dsp_arch' alongside O=3 can unlock additional optimization algorithms. The same O=3 mechanism (via 'finalize_config') applies to the HTP MCP (multicore) backend, using QNN_HTP_MCP_GRAPH_CONFIG_OPTION_FINALIZE_CONFIG / QnnHtpMcpGraph_CustomConfig_t. P points are an advanced O=3-only feature (no effect if O != 3): each P value selects a pre-defined point in the compiler's internal configuration space, trading off e.g. latency vs. DRAM bandwidth, baked into the context binary at offline-prepare time (no runtime flag needed, and execution output remains bit-accurate with a non-P-point build). Valid P values: 0 (default, no change), 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 13, 15, 16, 17, 19, 20, 21, 22, 23 (i.e. 0-23 excluding 7, 9, 10, 11, 12, 14, 18) — this valid set and each value's behavior may change release to release, so re-validate after any SDK upgrade. P points are unordered/independent (unlike O levels, a higher P number is not 'better'); specifying more than one P point in a config is undefined behavior; performance impact is model-specific (a P value that helps one model may do nothing or hurt another) — always validate functional correctness and performance on target hardware before deploying. Set via the backend-extension config's 'finalize_config': {'P': N} key inside graphs[] (HTP backend) or inside the graph_prepare.conf's per-graph entry (HTP MCP backend, alongside 'O': 3).

heading: HTP Performance Estimates
content: QNN can simulate target-hardware performance for a graph using per-operator cost/cycle predictions, producing lower/upper execution-cycle estimates and a confidence level — provided 'as is', not warranted for correctness/accuracy-vs-silicon/reliability, and may regress across releases; not to be relied on as accuracy error bounds. Estimates may not model custom-op costs, and assume burst mode with full uncontended DDR bandwidth (unrealistic if other SoC cores like CPU/GPU/camera are concurrently using DDR). Enable by setting the correct device 'soc_id' in the backend-extension config (e.g. soc_id 52 = SA8650/SA8775/SA8255) and passing --profiling_level basic to qnn-context-binary-generator, then reading results via qnn-profile-viewer --reader libQnnHtpProfilingReader.so (or libQnnHtpMcpProfilingReader.so for HTP MCP). Output includes Simulated (Accelerator exec cycles) plus lower/upper estimates, and per-HTP Bandwidth Stats in bytes: Input Fill (DDR reads for graph-input tensors with no predecessor), Intermediate Fill/Spill (compiler-generated same-HTP transfers to satisfy VTCM constraints), Inter HTP Fill/Spill (compiler-generated cross-HTP-core transfers), and Output Spill (DDR writes for graph-output tensors with no successor).

heading: HTP Forced Preemption
content: Preemption lets the OS pause a lower-priority client's graph execution and hand its VTCM/HMX resources to a higher-priority client (e.g. a safety/crash-detection graph preempting a low-priority speed-limit-detection graph); lower-priority graphs resume once all higher-priority graphs finish. Currently supported on SA8650, SA8775, SA8255, and SA8797. Usage assumption: each application process executes graphs of only one priority level (priority separation is enforced by the user, not the runtime) — higher-priority threads trigger immediate preemption of lower-priority clients. Demonstrated with two concurrent qnn-throughput-net-run instances (contexts.priority: 'LOW' vs 'HIGH' in each JSON config). Verify preemption occurred via qnn-profile-viewer's 'Backend (QNN Num times yield occurred): X count' output.

heading: Asynchronous Execution and Qmem Graph (Pointers)
content: Asynchronous graph execution (a QNN API implementation for async graph execution) has HTP-backend-specific details on the separate 'Asynchronous graph execution for HTP backend' page. Qmem Graph (shared_buffer only graph) is an HTP-backend-specific feature letting clients use data buffers for shared access between processing domains, eliminating host-CPU/HTP-accelerator data copies — see 'QNN HTP Qmem Graph'.

heading: HTP Session & Artifact Usage Guidelines
content: QNN supports only one set of libraries per process — all must be the same SDK version and matching Hexagon architecture version. Supported layout: one copy of each library (backend/stub/skel) per process; libQnnHtp.so should be dlopen'd explicitly rather than dynamically linked as a dependency; QNN searches for Stub.so/Prepare.so first alongside libQnnHtp.so, then in LD_LIBRARY_PATH; libraries should sit in the same directory (except the skel, if ADSP_LIBRARY_PATH correctly locates it); do not rename libraries to load multiple copies. Unsupported: multiple copies of libQnnHtp.so accessed in a single process (e.g. via a second dlopen from a different directory, or a duplicate filesystem via adb remount) — if detected at runtime, QNN_COMMON_ERROR_LOADING_BINARIES is returned from QnnBackend_registerOpPackage, QnnBackend_validateOpConfig, QnnContext_create, QnnDevice_create, and QnnGraph_create.

heading: Graph Switching (Beta)
content: Experimental feature trading execution speed for reduced sustained RAM: multiple graphs can be enabled but left unloaded, with only one graph fully loaded/ready at a time; executing an unloaded graph triggers automatic lazy-unload-then-load ('graph switching'). Memory saved is roughly proportional to the total size of unloaded models; first execution after a switch is slower. Limitations: no concurrent execution between switching graphs; incompatible with QNN_HTP_CONTEXT_CONFIG_OPTION_SHARE_RESOURCES. Enable via QnnContext_Config_t options QNN_CONTEXT_CONFIG_PERSISTENT_BINARY (isPersistentBinary=1) plus QNN_CONTEXT_CONFIG_MEMORY_LIMIT_HINT (any non-zero value) passed to QnnContext_createFromBinary, or via backend-extension config context_configs: {is_persistent_binary, memory_limit_hint, enable_graphs: [...]}. enable_graphs (optional) lists which graphs are enabled — only the first listed graph loads initially; if omitted, all graphs in the binary are enabled and the first graph in the binary loads first. When is_persistent_binary is set, the client should mmap the binary passed to QnnContext_createFromBinary, later call madvise() to release held pages, keep the mmap alive for the context's whole lifetime (never unmap before QnnContext_free), and is responsible for freeing the mmapped buffer and any spill-fill/weights buffers at context destruction to avoid leaks/undefined behavior.

heading: Multi-Graph Switching (Beta)
content: Enhances Graph Switching by keeping multiple graphs loaded/retained at once (vs. always unloading the current graph before loading the next), balancing memory and execution efficiency. Requires the same is_persistent_binary=true and memory_limit_hint>0 as traditional graph switching, plus a new 'graph_retention_order' config: an ordered list of graph names defining retention priority — graphs earlier in the list have higher priority, and as many are preloaded at deserialization as fit the PD's virtual address space; graphs not in the list are lazily loaded on demand; under memory pressure, non-retained graphs are always evicted first, then the least-important retained graph. graph_retention_order can be updated dynamically post-deserialization via QnnContext_setConfig with QNN_CONTEXT_CONFIG_GRAPH_RETENTION_ORDER — the new list fully replaces the old one but only takes effect on subsequent switches (does not immediately unload currently-loaded graphs); an empty list disables retention and falls back to traditional graph switching. Worked example with 4 equal-size graphs A/B/C/D (memory holds at most 2) and retention_order=[A,B]: repeated A,B,A,B executions incur zero switching overhead once A and B are both preloaded; execution order A,B,C,D evicts B (least-important retained) to fit C, then evicts C (non-retained, evicted before any retained graph) to fit D — illustrating that even retained graphs can be evicted under pressure, but non-retained graphs are always evicted first. Limitations: same as traditional graph switching (no concurrent execution between switching graphs, incompatible with QNN_HTP_CONTEXT_CONFIG_OPTION_SHARE_RESOURCES) plus higher RAM usage than traditional switching (more graphs stay loaded).

heading: Benefits of Batch Inference and Multi-Threaded Inference
content: Multi-threading hides CPU-HTP RPC communication latency — in single-threaded inference the RPC time of one call is dead time, but in multi-threaded inference the first thread's RPC time is masked by the second thread's inference, reducing HTP idle time. Batching multiple inferences improves weight-memory reuse (avoids reloading weights), though the benefit depends on activation size: low-resolution models (weights+activations fit in VTCM) get strong reuse efficiency from batching; large-resolution models see reduced reuse efficiency because activations consume more VTCM space, leaving less room to reuse weights across the batch. Measured example: SNPE 2.9 on SM8550 (Kailua.LA.1.1-00190 GKI.HY11-1) with snpe-parallel-run on ResNet50 — 1 batch/1 thread achieves ~1500 inf/s baseline; 5-batch/2-thread achieves ~2800 inf/s, roughly 1.8x.

heading: Hexagon NPU Runtime Driver (HNRD, Windows Only)
content: HNRD is available for Windows-on-Snapdragon (Windows X Series) platforms, forward/backward compatible with Qualcomm AI Stack SDKs, packaged/distributed independently of the QNN SDK (bundled with the device BSP by OEMs). Lets applications unbundle QNN HTP platform-dependent libraries so the same app binary is portable across older/newer Windows platforms. Pre-driver: only the traditional path exists (app bundles QNN HTP platform-dependent libs, e.g. QnnHtpV73Stub.dll/QnnHtpV73Skel.so). Post-driver: both traditional and HNRD paths are available and selected automatically — if the app bundles the platform-dependent libs it defaults to the traditional path; if not, it falls back to the HNRD path (log evidence: 'Traditional path not available. Switching to user driver path' / 'HTP user driver is loaded. Switched to user driver path'); no build-step difference exists between the two paths, and this applies to both QNN and SNPE. Minimum QNN/SNPE version for HNRD compatibility is 2.22.2; traditional and HNRD paths can coexist on one platform, each app independently choosing. Context Binary Management: when using HNRD, online prepare and context-binary loading are HNRD's responsibility (platform-dependent); a binary from one HNRD version may not run, or may not use all capabilities, on a different HNRD version — compatibility must be checked every load since HNRD can be upgraded/downgraded independently of the app. QnnContext_createFromBinary() auto-checks compatibility (governed by QnnContext_BinaryCompatibilityType_t: STRICT fails sub-optimal binaries, PERMISSIVE bypasses the sub-optimality check); QnnContext_validateBinary does the same check without creating a context. On failure, recommended pattern: continue running the sub-optimal/original context while doing online re-prepare on a background thread (fast prepare at optimization level 1 first, then a slower level-3 prepare), swapping to the new context once ready; if online prepare itself fails, the likely cause is an HNRD too old for the model's features, requiring an HNRD upgrade.

heading: QnnContext_createFromBinaryListAsync API
content: Asynchronously initializes multiple context binaries (models) in a single API call; supported only on Mobile and Windows. Provides initialization-time optimization and resource sharing (runtime memory / HTP virtual-address-space optimization) driven by QNN_HTP_CONTEXT_CONFIG_OPTION_SHARE_RESOURCES and _SHARE_RESOURCES_OPTIMIZATION_TYPE. Per-graph and per-context init-completion notifications arrive (possibly before or after the API call returns) in no guaranteed order; contexts with multiple graphs get one notification per graph plus one for the context itself; valid handles are delivered via these notifications. Guidelines: use a single thread to call this API with no other concurrent QNN API calls (model init is internally parallelized across CPU/HTP by the driver — concurrent calls cause over-subscription and I/O memory allocation/mapping failures); initialization-time optimization is impacted if the runtime SDK version differs from the SDK version used to prepare the binaries (a binary prepared on SDK 2.42 stays 'Optimized' on a 2.42 runtime but is 'Impacted' on a 2.43 runtime; a binary prepared on 2.43 is 'Not Supported' on a 2.42 runtime); do not use QNN_HTP_CONTEXT_CONFIG_OPTION_REGISTER_MULTI_CONTEXTS or _REGISTER_CONCURRENT_RESOURCE_SHARING with this API (those are for QnnContext_createFromBinary only); when QNN_HTP_CONTEXT_CONFIG_OPTION_SHARE_RESOURCES is enabled, use Multi-Tensor shared I/O buffers and set graph batch size to 1 (avoids shared-buffer reallocation). Data Callback: by default QNN allocates memory and copies weight/section data from the binary; clients may instead register allocation/population callbacks via the 'callback' parameter in QnnContext_ParamsV2_t (see QnnContext_createFromBinaryWithCallback) for platform-specific efficient memory strategies.

heading: Multiple cDSP Sessions (Multi-PD)
content: Certain Snapdragon SoCs can open multiple cDSP sessions from one CPU application process (aka 'Multi-PD', though PD technically denotes a different transport concept). cDSP is a 32-bit processor: each session gets a 4GB virtual-address space, of which only 3.75GB is usable; max memory per CPU process is min(total free RAM, sessions-per-process x 3.75GB). Model loading: QnnContext_createFromBinary()/_createFromBinaryWithSignal()/_createFromBinaryListAsync() deserialize onto the first session with enough free space, trying subsequent sessions if it fails — models deserialized onto a non-first session incur slightly higher init time (backend tries all prior sessions sequentially first). A single context binary cannot span multiple sessions — clients must split large models before running QNN converter tools, with split points chosen based on network topology, precision (a8w4/a16w4/a8w8/a16w8/etc.), and per-session VA limit; despite the 3.75GB usable limit, it's recommended to keep any single context binary's heap under 3GB. The backend alone decides which session to use — there's no client-facing API to pick a session, and packing is 'first-fit' (worked example: 1.5GB+1.5GB+1GB+1GB+250MB models loaded sequentially — the first two 1.5GB models both fit on session #0 filling it to 3GB; the third (1GB) model doesn't fit on session #0 so spawns session #1; the fourth (1GB) fits on session #1 (now at 2GB); the fifth (250MB) fits back on session #0). I/O buffer mapping failures can occur (especially for large-I/O LLM/LVM use cases using QnnMem_register-registered shared buffers) when a session's VA space is packed too tightly to map I/O for a context deserialized there — an I/O buffer for a given context must map to the same session that context was deserialized onto. Mitigations: use QNN_HTP_CONTEXT_CONFIG_OPTION_IO_MEM_ESTIMATION (requires sequential model init, each model's I/O registered right after that model inits; raises peak RAM during init but not sustained RAM); register a 'dummy' memory buffer to deliberately force a context onto the next session, freeing the dummy afterward; or use QnnContext_createFromBinaryListAsync (which can be enabled with VA-space optimization so the backend reserves I/O mapping space across a single batched init call).

heading: Enabling Async Init on Older Context Bins
content: To allow async init on a context binary, use either the QnnContext_createFromBinaryListAsync API, or pass QNN_HTP_CONTEXT_CONFIG_OPTION_INIT_ACCELERATION to QnnContext_createFromBinary — and the binary's version must match the runtime SDK's version. If versions mismatch, a read-write-accessible patch-file path must be supplied via QNN_HTP_CONTEXT_CONFIG_OPTION_CONCURRENT_DESERIALIZATION_PATCH; backend-extension config equivalents are context.init_acceleration (bool, required only for createFromBinary — not needed with createFromBinaryListAsync, default false) and context.concurrent_deserialization_patch (a read-writeable path; on the first mismatched run patch data is measured and stored there, and subsequent runs reuse it to enable async init — the first run does not see the async-init speedup, only later runs do).

heading: Init and Execute Cancellation
content: Two cancellation signal types: Abort Signal (client-triggered interrupt) and Timeout Signal (automatic interrupt after a predefined period). QnnContext_createFromBinaryWithSignal lets clients interrupt context creation using either signal type; QnnGraph_execute can likewise be interrupted by either signal type. See QnnSignal.h for signal config details.

heading: Multicore
content: QNN HTP supports multicore graph execution on supported SoCs (single-core is the default otherwise). Offline preparation: set 'num_cores' (default 1) per-graph in the backend-extension config to compile a graph to run across N cores. Runtime device creation: call QnnDevice_getPlatformInfo() to learn per-device core count/IDs/types, then populate a QnnDevice_Config_t (QNN_DEVICE_CONFIG_OPTION_PLATFORM_INFO) with the desired core set and call QnnDevice_create(). Multicore Performance Infrastructure requires voting separately per core: call perfInfra.createPowerConfigId(deviceId, coreId, ...) once per core to get distinct power-config IDs, then call perfInfra.setPowerConfig(powerConfigId[i], ...) for each. Example backend-extension config for a 2-core case (core_id [1,2], core_type 0/NSP) sets perf_profile e.g. 'burst' under devices[].cores[]. Limitations — the following are NOT supported for multicore graphs: multicore concurrency, linting profile, cancellation of context-create-from-binary, graph switching/selection, spill-fill buffer sharing, weight sharing.

heading: Graph Priority and Setting Graph Priority
content: Setting a QNN priority level X assigns HMX threads priority Y and HVX threads priority Y+1 (a HIGHER numeric value means LOWER execution priority). QNN-to-HTP-thread-priority mapping: QNN_PRIORITY_LOW/LOWEST(0)→HMX 0xC5/HVX 0xC6; QNN_PRIORITY_NORMAL_LOW(50)→0xC3/0xC4; QNN_PRIORITY_NORMAL/DEFAULT(100)→0xC0/0xC1; QNN_PRIORITY_NORMAL_HIGH(150)→0xBD/0xBE; QNN_PRIORITY_HIGH(200)→0xBB/0xBC; QNN_PRIORITY_HIGH_PLUS(300)→0xB6/0xB7; QNN_PRIORITY_CRITICAL(400)→0xB1/0xB2; QNN_PRIORITY_CRITICAL_PLUS/HIGHEST(500)→0xAC/0xAD. Set at QnnGraph_create time via QnnGraph_Config_t with QNN_GRAPH_CONFIG_OPTION_PRIORITY (default QNN_PRIORITY_DEFAULT if unset); modify an existing graph's priority via QnnGraph_setConfig (some priority levels may be restricted for general developers depending on the device); QnnContext_setConfig with QNN_CONTEXT_CONFIG_OPTION_PRIORITY updates the priority of every graph in that context at once.

heading: LLM Native KVcache
content: Native KVcache avoids the expensive per-inference transformation of KV tensors into HMX layout (a cost that grows with context length) by keeping the KV cache resident in HMX layout directly, managed by the LLM pipeline's KV-management module. Benefits: improved TTFT and token rate especially at large context lengths; reduced power, especially at large context lengths. Constraints: only applies to GenAI LLM models with KV-cache mode; KV-cache tensors must be uint8 and symmetrically quantized; native KV-cache must be all-or-nothing across decoder layers (no partial adoption); context length must be a multiple of 256 and attention head_dim a multiple of 64; the KV-concat op in attention must be ScatterElement, not Concat; for AR-N where N is a multiple of 32, KV input must be native (output can be native or flat, native output further speeds kvupdate during TTFT); for AR-N where N is not a multiple of 32 (typically AR1/4/8/16), KV input must be native, KV output must be flat, and scatter_index <= (context_length - roundup(N,32)) — the client must convert flat output back to native input itself in this case. Enable by exporting a compliant model (per the QC notebook) and specifying dataFormat = QNN_TENSOR_DATA_FORMAT_HMX_WEIGHT_LAYOUT for the relevant KV I/O tensors via qnn-context-binary-generator's --data_format_config JSON (all KV tensors must be listed if enabling both native input and output; a subset of tensors if only native input is wanted). QNN_TENSOR_DATA_FORMAT_HMX_WEIGHT_LAYOUT is currently only supported in this native-KVcache scenario. Layout transform for a single head's flat [DIN, DOUT] shape: [DIN,DOUT] -> DOUT/KV_TILE_SIZE * [DIN, KV_TILE_SIZE] -> DOUT/KV_TILE_SIZE * [DIN/32, KV_TILE_SIZE/32, 8:DIN, 32:KV_TILE_SIZE, 4:DIN] -> [DOUT/KV_TILE_SIZE, DIN*KV_TILE_SIZE/1024, 1024], with fixed tile sizes in this SDK version: K_TILE_SIZE=256, V_TILE_SIZE=64. Other limitations: cannot support v68 or lower targets; max context length is capped by fixed VTCM size (e.g. 2MB VTCM won't support 16K+ context length).

heading: MaskedSoftmax
content: Replaces the Softmax(Add(In, Mask)) pattern in LLM attention blocks, using the attention_mask tensor to mask out invalid tokens directly in the softmax op rather than via a preceding Add. Benefits: improved LLM accuracy; improved TTFT and token rate, especially when valid tokens are a small fraction of a large context length. Constraint: currently only applicable to uint16 and uint8 quantization. To enable, the model must match one of three supported structural patterns (A/B/C), differing in attention_mask dtype and Where-op condition polarity: pattern A/B quantize attention_mask to uint16 (pattern A keeps zero-valued mask entries and masks non-zero entries via an Equal op producing the Where condition; pattern B masks zero-valued entries and keeps non-zero via a NotEqual op); pattern C quantizes attention_mask to uint8 with the same zero-masked/non-zero-kept polarity as pattern B. In all patterns the Where op masks invalid entries of the Softmax input tensor (false condition → 0 output for that entry after softmax; true condition → normal softmax). An Add(ReduceMin(Input), B) structure avoids quantization/dequantization loss, with B (the Add operand) constrained to <= -20.0 to guarantee the invalid-entry softmax output is 0.

heading: QnnContext_createFromBinaryWithCallback API
content: Lets clients register callback-based buffer allocation and data-loading strategies for synchronous context creation, overriding QnnContext_createFromBinary's default internal-allocate-and-copy behavior. The Qnn_ContextBinaryCallback_t structure carries a data-provider callback (Qnn_ContextBinaryDmaDataProviderFn_t / _RawDataProviderFn_t) and a data-release callback (_DmaDataReleaseFn_t / _RawDataReleaseFn_t); the provider callback must ensure the requested section's buffer is allocated and fully populated before returning. Two callback types exist: RawBufferCallback_t (standard user-space buffers, not shared with the backend) and DmaBufferCallback_t (DMA buffers, directly shareable with the backend for zero-copy loading) — currently only DmaBufferCallback_t is implemented (RawBufferCallback_t is reserved for future use), and it's currently used only for model-weights loading ('external weights-loaded buffer' feature). Key benefit: with a direct-I/O scheme, this avoids a user-space-to-backend-buffer runtime copy, reducing memory and time overhead (actual gain depends on the client's callback implementation). Buffer requirements: DMA buffers only when using the DMA callback type; start address must be 4KB/page-aligned, with dataStartOffset ideally exactly 0 (context binaries from qairt-2.37+ 4KB-align the main data section for this reason; the backend has limited support for arbitrary offsets); buffer must be at least the requested size; each dataProvider invocation must return a distinct FD; the buffer must not be modified by anything other than QNN after loading, and must not be deallocated until the dataRelease callback fires. Limitations: requires qairt-2.37+ context binaries for the direct-I/O alignment guarantees; incompatible with graph switching, the udma64 feature, QNN_CONTEXT_CONFIG_OPTION_DEFER_GRAPH_INIT, and securePD model protection; validated on Android only (other platforms need additional adaptation). On Windows-on-Snapdragon (WoS), callbacks can instead memory-map the on-disk context binary (via CreateFileMappingA/MapViewOfFile plus libcdsprpc.dll's remote_register_buf_attr2/rpcmem_to_fd) to reduce system commit charge, share one physical-memory copy across multiple instances of the same model, and let the OS reclaim memory without swap writes — read-only memory mapping depends on NPU-driver/OS support (falls back to QNN_CONTEXT_ERROR_UNSUPPORTED_FEATURE, retry without callbacks), only DMA callbacks are supported, callbacks require context-binary version 3.3.3+ (older binaries return QNN_CONTEXT_ERROR_INVALID_CONFIG), and this memory-mapping approach is WoS-only.

heading: QNN HTP Monolithic LSTM
content: Executes LSTM as a single monolithic unit instead of the default behavior of expanding it into many smaller sub-operations. Config: QnnHtpGraph_CustomConfig_t option QNN_HTP_GRAPH_CONFIG_OPTION_MONOLITHIC_LSTM (monolithicLstm bool), or the offline backend-extension 'monolithic_lstm' key (default false/disabled either way). Recommended use: enable for multi-step LSTMs with large time-step counts (e.g. 2000) and small depth (e.g. <=256) — reduces prepare time and context-binary size in this regime; disable (expand) for single-step LSTM, or for multi-step LSTM with large depth (>256, where memory becomes the execution-time bottleneck and monolithic mode may increase execute time). Requires a compatible Hexagon NPU Runtime Driver: an outdated driver silently skips the optimization at prepare time, but if a binary already prepared with Monolithic LSTM is later run against an outdated driver, execution fails outright (unsupported feature).

heading: Multi-SoC DLC with Reference Weight Sharing
content: Enables offline preparation of context binaries for multiple target SoCs embedded into a single DLC file. Reference weight sharing reduces ROM footprint by storing one shared weight blob referenced by all the SoC-specific context binaries, instead of duplicating the weights per SoC. Full details are on the separate 'Multi-SoC DLC with Reference Weight Sharing' page.
